text: The surgeon identifies the ureter and applies medial retraction to move it out of the operating field, preparing for lateral pelvic lymph node dissection.
The surgeon dissects the ureter along with the ureterohypogastric fascia, establishing the medial margin of the dissection.
The surgeon focuses on the external iliac vessels, preparing to dissect around these structures in the pelvic region.
The surgeon dissects lymphofatty tissues from the surface of the external iliac artery and vein, carefully separating these tissues from the vascular structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection deeper laterally until the obturator internus muscles are visible, establishing the lateral margin of the pelvic lymph node dissection.
The surgeon performs deeper dissection, revealing the obturator nerve situated between the bifurcation of the external and internal iliac veins.
The obturator nerve is visually identified and preserved during the dissection, unless it is visibly involved by metastatic lymph nodes.
The surgeon dissects and ligates the root of the obturator artery, which bifurcates from the internal iliac artery, visible within the pelvic region.
The surgeon ligates the proximal end of the obturator vein, completing the dissection in the pelvic region.
The surgeon ligates the distal end of the obturator vein, completing the ligation process of this vessel within the pelvic region.
The surgeon performs dissection of the lymphofatty tissue along the obturator nerve, progressing towards the obturator foramen, with the nerve visibly preserved amidst the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon ligates the distal end of the obturator artery, completing the vascular control in the region.
The surgeon completes the ligation of the obturator artery and vein, achieving full clearance of lymphatic tissue within the obturator space.
The surgeon continues the dissection distally along the obturator nerve, progressing towards the obturator foramen, ensuring complete clearance of lymphofatty tissue.
The deep circumflex iliac vein is identified, marking the most caudal part of the dissection in the anterior region.
The surgeon performs dissection along the vesico-hypogastric plane, revealing the internal iliac artery within the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects the branches of the anterior internal iliac artery, carefully separating them from surrounding tissue within the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects and ligates the umbilical artery at its root, completing the procedure within the pelvic region.
The surgeon carefully dissects and preserves the superior vesicle artery, ensuring its integrity during the procedure.
The surgeon evaluates the branches of the internal iliac artery, indicating that they can be sacrificed if necessary during the dissection.
The surgeon dissects lymphatic tissue located between the internal iliac vein and the ureterohypogastric fascia, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon dissects and ligates the inferior vesicle artery at its root, completing the process of isolating this vessel within the pelvic region.
The surgeon performs dissection between the bladder and the lymphatic tissue, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon ligates the distal vesicle artery and veins at the bladder side using an advanced energy device or hemlock clips, ensuring secure closure of these vessels.
The surgeon dissects the most caudal part of the lymphatic tissue beyond the vesicle vessels, carefully separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgical field reveals a space bounded by the lateral pelvic sidewall, levator ani muscles, and medial pelvic plexuses, providing a clear view of the anatomical boundaries within the pelvis.
The surgeon places the resected specimen into a retrieval bag and removes it from the surgical field.
The surgeon achieves complete clearance of the lateral pelvic lymph nodes while preserving the hypogastric nerve plexus by ligating intersecting branches and veins of the internal iliac artery.
